And there is no there is no definitive way, frankly, I mean, customer acquisition cost. They’re sort of his average order value there sort of is but even that has some gray area lifetime value. Certainly there is no, there’s no predetermined lifetime. And when I ran an agency, I had a client for 21 years, a single client for 21 years, my first client, and who was my also my last client, I had another for 20 years, many for 15 Plus, so what was the lifetime value? Well, you don’t know going into it. Other companies like the large financial publishers, Motley Fool agora clock, places like that, their business models generally are set up on a 30 day ARV. So the whole business is set up on, there’s a cost to acquire the customer 30 days for to establish, or average order value, and then six months to establish lifetime, customer life, lifetime customer value. Now, that’s a very different business in those taking that approach creates very different outcomes than saying to yours, for example, we talked about Dean Jackson’s research a lot, which, you know, when you sort of know how the world works, in digital marketing, you realize these beams, like it like he and I, our friend, Dean Jackson’s data is very accurate, which is when you have a large sampling of buyers, the that only about 15 to 20% of the people who are eventually going to buy buy in the first 90 days, the remainder by up to two years. So I like the two year window that feels kind of right. But it’s again, it’s not set in stone. And that was my process. And that was really fun. And I did that over and over and over again. But for somebody else, you know, creating that front end is maybe the most difficult challenging things that’s not well, probably don’t start there then start somewhere else. Shawn 22:50
That’s funny, I didn’t I’m glad I let you answer this first, because I it took me a while to actually figure out my answer. This is such a great strategy on my part, I just love this. What occurred to me is I, I have a skill set. That to me seems it’s natural as it breathing. But it is a separate skill set. And it’s because what I had to do working with clients, is it within a day or two? I had to be able to answer this question for them. Yeah, sometimes it was a week or more. But I had to be able to sit down with a client and ask a bunch of questions. And then from those questions, create a strategy that would move their business forward. Because ultimately, at the end of the day, that’s that’s the only thing that mattered. It wasn’t my winning personality or charm. It was did I get some results? Yes or no? So there is a skill here. And I really want I want to figure out a way to teach this system that is I’m really excited about thing, as I think about it. And that skill is how do you become so adept at the story of your business? That this and this question essentially answers itself all the time? So let me unpack that a little bit. Hope that makes a little more sense. I’ve said a million times I probably said this in the masterclass too, that if you have to be able to truly understand your business, you have to be able to draw on a piece of a single sheet of paper with a crayon, like like, where’s my traffic come from? What happens when I get that traffic? And what happens as a result all the way to whatever the system is my business system, what does it work? What does it doing? And when you become the it’s not, it’s not a spreadsheet. This is where I think people go awry. They put a bunch of numbers in a spreadsheet, and they’re like, I still don’t know what to do. And it’s not. It’s because this the spreadsheet doesn’t tell a story. I’ll use an example that just popped into my head from this is quite a while ago, I worked for a large culinary school, doing lead gen and they were in a market of like an ultra competitive market for lead gen because there were lead aggregators, they were the lead aggregator or people who buy, they use they use Google search in their buying people who are looking For culinary schools in general, and then they can pay a ton because they resell those leads to culinary schools. And when you’re competing against them, the economics are very different because one lead for them can be sold 1015 2030 times, whereas for an individual culinary school that the needs to convert from them, so the economics are wildly different. So I was working with them in that capacity. And for them, it was always we just need more leads, more leads. But at some point, I sat down and said, Okay, let’s, let’s look at the story that’s being told here. And the story that was being told them in their data was that the program that they were most interested in building was their online program. And their online program was getting, I think it was somewhere in the neighborhood of 400, or 450 visits, unique visits visitors per week.

But they were there, the number of applicants was ridiculously low. And as we were in this strategy meeting, and I opened up the page and looking at the problem, it was all conversion problems. Like it was just, it didn’t make once you found that page, and you found the info about the online program, it was almost impossible to submit an application like nobody ever actually sat down and tried to go through the whole process. And I was trying to do it. In this meeting, the strategy meeting, to figure out like, what, like, what’s the problem here? And what was clearly a problem, the story that was being told, was, they’re getting a lot of traffic, but they’re but the system doesn’t convert that traffic in any capacity to customer. So it’s a massive barrier, and their solution was more traffic. And what what my point was, that when you have a conversion problem more traffic is never the answer. But but you have to understand that the buy point is the specific deployment is the ability to, to look at your business and and know the story that it’s telling. It’s a lift in. And this is not easy. But it is there’s some of the dimensions that we talked about in the masterclass around, you need to understand your audience. And when we say your audience, we mean that that means your audience, if you have an email list, that’s part of your audience, the audience, you have organic traffic, that’s part of your audience. Maybe you do, you know, you’re getting some referral traffic, because you’re on podcasts occasionally, that’s part of your audience. Any traffic that comes to any page on the internet over which you have control your Facebook page, let’s say you have a large organic audience on Facebook, that’s your audience, right? You need to understand that and you need to have somewhere in your head, or even better on a piece of paper to sit down and say, okay, in the last 30 days, here’s what my audience looks like, I got 200. Let’s just say I’ve got 500 people in a Facebook group, who I interact with in this way. And I have 10,000 people on an email list or 1000, whatever it is, but make you have to know that, right? Because if you don’t know, if you have no idea what the map looks like, the hell do you know where to go? Right, like, and what most people do is like, oh, yeah, I know what’s going on my business. I just need to figure out like, what the next big thing is, it’s like, okay, great. What is your cost to acquire a customer? I don’t know, how many people here what percentage, what percentage of people who become customers come from organic? Oh, I don’t know. How many leads did you get in the last 30 days? I’m not sure. It’s like, well, how the hell do you know where to go? If you don’t know what those basic pieces of information, and I’m not suggesting Mary doesn’t, I’m just, I’m already in read stage, which happened faster than I thought it would. But you have to know the story that your business is telling. Because once you know that story, when you do something, the story changes a little bit. Now you know, something new, right? So let’s just say for the sake of argument that you the story of your business is they overwhelmingly you get organic traffic over which you don’t have control. And the quality of that trend, like the the quality of the traffic is great, because they consume all of your content, but but they don’t you’re not monetizing them. So lots of traffic, no monetization. Where’s the opportunity? The opportunity is in monetization, right? You’ve got a boatload of traffic, right? Don’t try to because this is what happens a lot. Someone’s like well, I have a ton of organic traffic. But I don’t so I’m so worried on Google’s want to do this or Google’s going to do that and take great worry about that later when you’re that traffic is generating revenue for you. Right the opportunity when you have lots of traffic and no offer is to create an offer. The opportunity when you have no traffic in an offer is to get traffic like that those offers you have to know that story. I’m gonna stop ranting My blood pressure is already up. But anything to contribute to that before we we jump into whatever’s next year I can sure what’s next.

André 32:22
Yeah, so when I first created this idea, it was they needed to be contrast. So the contrast came through painting a picture of Frank who represented who represented a portion of our audience, a large portion, and then met now Matt represented somebody who some people would be a match. How much you guys incorporating paid ads and your systems? Could you run through any recent success stories? So we did. We ran paid traffic on Facebook paid traffic last year. I can’t remember the exact dates, I just pulled open wicked reports to see if I can find the dates. So for us, it’s there’s a kind of it’s funny, we so we turn traffic off in. I believe it was November 21. Because we weren’t really, we didn’t want to run traffic, we had already made a decision. We were putting everything together as into the system at that point. We didn’t want to and we didn’t really particularly want to run traffic in December when everybody when costs are skyrocketing because everybody’s trying to advertise. We have not we have not read. We haven’t read done that. That’s on the the agenda for later. And I’ll actually hit that just a little bit a second. But the difference with us and I want to be really, really clear about this, that we there too. And I’ve done a lot of paid traffic. And there are two ways to think about it. And they’re all around when when does customer acquisition cost and average order value? When do those two things meet up? We don’t have a front end offer in our business, meaning we do not have an offer that is designed to acquire customers to offset the cost of the acquisition of those customers. Because of that, we needed to think about paid traffic entirely differently, which is we needed to take a financial risk, saying we’re willing to spend x amount of dollars to get however many people that brings into our world and we We know that the monetization of that of that ad spend is going to occur essentially in two ways that we’re going to pay attention to. The first is over 90 days. So what what happens over 90 days, and because again, we we do what we teach. So we know that we’re going to get some, somewhere in the neighborhood of 15 to 20% of the initial sales we’re going to get in that first 90 days. But what we really care about and why we have to use a tool like wicked reports, because it’s one of the few tools that will do this is we then also need to be able to look at that group of ad spend that money spent in the last year and track it over two years, because that’s going to tell us, it’s going to show that how we’re doing what we’re doing. How that plays out over the appropriate timeframe, not over 30 days, we’re not in a three, we’re not in a 30 day optimization business. So I mentioned all of that, just to be very cautious about taking any lesson from what we’re doing, and applying it to what you’re doing. Because if the underlying structure of what you’re trying to accomplish is not the same, you should not take anything from how we’re approaching this at all. So, you know, we we have seen, probably in the neighborhood of what I would have expected to see on that on the on the 90 day window. But then, of course, we introduced this entire like, we completely have turned the entire system on its head. So we had four core courses, we were bringing people into a relationship building series that emphasize all four of those were, and then all of a sudden, we don’t have four courses. And we have this one thing. So it’s We’ve very much changed the playing field, I still expect the economics of that to work out well over the timeframe that we that we anticipated, the real change for us is going to be post

I’m guessing it’s probably going to be May, maybe June, once we get into June, it’s summer, then summer sucks from a sales perspective. So I don’t I don’t know what happens if we get too close to Jim. But what we in broad strokes, what we knew this year was going to look like is we were going to move as many people of our existing customers to the system as possible first to create a critical mass of people in the system. We’ve done that that happened in early February, we knew after that the two things that we were doing. The two big things that we were doing is launching TLB lab in which that’s going to happen at the end of March March 31 through April 7 will be the actual promotion time we’ll do pls before that, there’s going to be a founder’s discount, which is just absolutely ridiculous. Founders discount and at the same time, we’re also doing TLB elite, which is our high level group coaching. That that all we planned all of that to happen first that those were the series of priorities. Once that is in place on the other side of that is where we we are going to shift our focus to sort of two tracks primarily, broadly speaking, speaking, one track is building out the system and we’re in creating the content from for lab and working with our group in elite. Like that’s a big track. But the other track is we need to create a specific front end offer for our business that we can use to monetize the acquisition of customers through paid traffic. I’m sure we’ll do some case studies stuff about how we’re thinking about that in the system. Lots of that will happen. But in the absence of having an offer that monetizes traffic, pay traffic’s a different animal. That’s a very different animal. So I don’t know if that’s the complete answer yet. I know, Scott gross, the gross LEA is the founder. He’s a dear friend of mine. I’ve worked with the news client for years. I know we can report backwards and forwards it does. The thing that wicked report does that other things don’t is calculate lifetime value over a very long period of time, and go back and assign that value to the original source of the lead. And that’s very uncommon. So we ran traffic campaigns last year that I will know the economics of for years ahead. And that is critical for our business understanding pay traffic, if your business model doesn’t require that if your business model works on a customer accurate, shorter time frame, a 30 day customer acquisition window, where you have a lower cost offer that you can monetize that traffic in 30 days and see it in tools like Google Analytics, great. I’m not I’m not advocating for wicked reports. When you do consider a higher level program I happen to love wicked reports. Happy to talk with anybody individually about that. Shawn 1:00:54
Nice answer. All right. It’s nothing for a tiny marketing agency that uses the system. Do you have any recommendations for how to get clients to engage in questions and answer sessions, for example, empathy, map chain of beliefs, questions related to world building on a level of deep enough to produce answers that I can work with threats of violence, it’s not about that. Bring a hammer and bring it all do this, Andre first and then hand it off to you. Because I’ve dealt with this. For two decades, I dealt with this, right? Like, clients are funny. They want outcomes. In my experience client, it’s off the relationship between the outcomes caught clients work, or sorry, the relationship between the outcome clients want in the work they’re willing to do, does not appear to have any correlation. It’s not like, hey, I want you to go do magic things. And I’m not really going to help you get there for not every client, but sometimes a large number of clients. So to specifically answer your question, I think you have to be the expert in the adult in the room. And that means and it’s a great way from positioning yourself as the expert.

But to be very clear, the way you do things get better results than the alternatives, assuming that’s true. So you can do the work that you’ve had the experience and the expertise and that you can deliver, if you can say that it’s not bragging if you can do it, if you can say that. And you need to be able to sit across from a client and say, in order to get the best results from this project, I’m going to need you to engage with me on a series of whatever activities or a series of research, whatever it is. And doing that is critical to the success of this enterprise. If we just hit the high notes, we don’t really get into it, we’re not going to get the best result. And I will not be able to deliver the best result for you. And my integrity as a professional demands that I get the best possible result for you as my client. Like once you’ve made that framing you as soon as the client steps into that frame, you own the frame. So anytime the client, if the client buys into that they’re like, Okay, got it, we understand the moment that happens. Anytime you meet resistance in the future, you go back to that frame. So when the client says, you know, we don’t have time to do empathy, empathy map, but we did this exercise three years ago, and I can send you the Google doc to it. You the moment you say yes to that you’re training your client, not to respect what you said. That’s when you have a full stop. And this takes this is hard. And believe me, I’ve been in situations where I needed the client for financial reasons, and I didn’t want to do this, but it will cause you endless frustration and pain. If you acquiesce to this the moment you say to the client, I need x and they say, Okay, well, we’ll do this, instead, it will be just as good. You stop right then and you go back to the frame that you said, and you say, Listen, I, I appreciate that this work is difficult and time consuming, and that there are other things you would want to be doing. However, in the beginning of this relationship, I made it clear to you that my integrity demands I get the best results on your behalf. A three year old document that your team put together is there is not what I need to do my job well, I need us to do this exercise in order and if you’re not willing to do that, that I can’t deliver the best from you. And that’s not acceptable to me. So when should we schedule the time to do this exercise properly? Like that’s what professionals do. It’s scary as hell and you will have clients that tell you to go pound sand, right? You know, clients are saying no, and guess what those are, there’s a name for them. They’re called former clients. Right now if you need the cash and I get it, it’s not always that easy. But then, if you if you absolutely have to have the cash and you have it and you think that relationship is salvageable. In that same situation, you can say, Listen, in order to do my best work for you, I really need us to go through this exercise. If for some reason that’s truly not possible for you to do And I need to use this, whatever three year old document, I will look at that document and see if I can extract from what I need from, I will do my best to make that happen. But I may have additional questions for you, who should be responsible for answering those questions. And then you just you go do the work and you come back to them, but then you haven’t really given up too much ground. Because the deal with clients is they will treat you the way you train them to treat you. And if you say one thing and but you’re willing to, to always acquiesce to their demands that you become the the service provider who does that, and they’ll, they’ll drive a train through that open door, that’s just human nature doesn’t make them bad people just That’s how people work. But when you half of your job, working with clients is to train your clients to be good clients, and to hold the line. And it’s hard as hell. But it’s, believe me, it’s worth it, especially if you’re just getting started. And you want to attract good clients. Because when you when you act like that, when you’re a professional, and you sit there and you show up as a professional, you will attract clients who are willing to pay for professionals, because most of the world are full of dumb asses are not professionals, especially in a service business. Every asshat in the world thinks they can go out and start a Facebook ads marketing agency tomorrow, right? And that’s, that’s 80% of who you’re dealing with. Are those people don’t be them. Right, be the person who says this is what’s required to do this work, right. And that’s the only thing I’m going to do because I have a fiduciary responsibility to you as my client to get the best result. And you’re either going to help me with that, or I’m gonna go find another client. Because that’s what I do. That’s, that’s where you want to be. Anyway, all right, Andre, you want to do the nice version of that, like, Good cop, bad cop? Bad Cop? Meeting patient cop, there’s really no fucks to get like, we can’t see that. Sorry. I’ll edit that out of the, the the transcript? And the No, the no something alternative to give cop cakes Damn it. Right. What do you want to add to that?

André 1:07:01
Yeah, I don’t really have much to say, at the end of 2019. When mentioned the client, but a really big company approached short and asked to do work for them. So although it wasn’t necessarily an agency type arrangement, it was us just strategizing with them. They were familiar with our work. And so they approached us within that frame, they had recognized that their style of very aggressive marketing was atrophying, you know. And they needed, they needed to try different things. So they were completely open with coming on board and do exactly what we said that eventually turned into into a bit of an agency component. But, you know, they went and did the audience enough a masterclass exercises, you know, because that’s, that’s, that’s part of the stuff we do. So when we said, you know, go do this, these, these are the questions that you need to bring back to us, at the end of the week that went into those there was there was no, there was no question, yes, none of that, they just went and did the work. And I think if I, if I, if I ever had an agency, it would be, you know, this is this is this is our, this is our expertise, and this is what we do. And if you, if you’re looking for that, then that frame kind of establishes the whole front end conversation and end up, you know, recognize that most agencies aren’t like that. It’s a bit more messy, but when, when, when Sean and I open elite, shortly, that’s essentially going, you know, Shawn, and I have a certain bunch of expertise that is wrapped up in and codified in inside of the system, you know, all our stuff that we that we currently do and have been doing for four decades is wrapped up and codified. And it’s available to everybody inside of the system. And when we open up elite, those people, that’s it’s almost going to be although it’s not an agency thing, because we’re not doing the work for them, but we’re going to be helping people make sense of this and, and, and go and do the work and, and be be be part of their feedback loop. When they when they come come up against you know, you know, obstacles along the way, which is which is which is normal. So I think, again, that kind of answers that in in a sideways sort of way. But I think if you have a certain this is this is what we do. And it’s very clear. And if that’s not what you want, then I’m sorry, we can’t take you on as a client. And that solves that problem. But again, I’m saying that for for someone that’s never really been in the client business.

Shawn 1:10:00
I think what I’ll I’ll give everybody my favorite gift to wrap up this question too, which is, you know, we set out in places I love questions. This is this is this is this is really this is the best question in the world for clients. But it’s also a great question for teams. It’s a great question. If you’re a solopreneur, it’s the best. It’s my favorite question. I think questions are like children, though, I have so many that are favorites. This is the one that I’ve used so many times. The question is very simple. What are we optimizing for? So when a client comes to you, and they hire you, like, oh, you can get these results, and we’ve heard great things about you. Excellent. And then say, okay, one of the first things we need to do is we need to create an empathy map. By the way, that noise you can hear in the backgrounds of snowplow going by my house, there’s so much snow, it’s ridiculous. So if we say, Alright, for this client, you say, okay, great, we’re gonna need to do this exercise and create an opportunity map. 90 minutes from your three people on your team, they go, that’s gonna be hard. You know, we’ve got this, we’ve got the same we could do. We kind of did this a while ago, we can just give you that. That’s a perfect opportunity to stop and say, Okay, I want to make sure I understand what’s happening here. I want to know exactly what is what in this relationship? What are we optimizing for here? Are we optimizing for this result that you’ve hired before? That you want to do whatever increase sales by 20%? Over XYZ?

Are we optimizing for that? Are we optimizing for extra for what’s easy? Because the moment you pose that question to somebody, you’re, everything stops? And they’re like, Well, no, no, no, we want, we want this result. Great. Well, in order to get that result, we need to do the damn work. Right? Like that. That’s the connection, there’s actually a causal connection between the result you want and the activities you do Oh, my God, who knew? Right? Don’t don’t bring this attitude to the party, because it pisses people off. But that’s the that’s the voice that I hear in my head. And that’s really that’s the question to ask all the time, all the time. What are we optimizing for? So when you sit down, and there’s always going to be some asshat in the marketing department who is optimizing for their own job security, and they don’t want to take risks, they don’t want to do anything new, because they have to go to the boss. And if there’s a mistake, or things don’t perform there, they’re terrified. They undermine everything that you do, or they don’t want you to look better than that. There are million things that can happen. And what are we optimizing for externalizes that stuff, right? And I’ve used this, I use this all the time, I would have my, you know, I had a client for a while, that was just a consultation thing. They were running Google search, and they were doing it so poorly, was an autoparts dealer thing, and they just gave every part of the business, the same amount of money. And the way that everybody wanted to get, you know, spread that money across a million things. And it just made no sense. And then I came in, and I was like, and I just did a very quick assessment. What, what are the top five products? Were they what are what are they? What are their most profitable? Can we spend all of your budget within an area that you could say free? free ground shipping? The answer was yes. So they went from like advertising to 100 products, only, of which only a few were actually selling, we re optimize the entire camp, the entire account in like hours to only focus on, I think it was four, maybe five, I can’t remember exactly. A limited number of products that sold really well. The conversion rates were outstanding, from some factors on price or whatever. We only advertise those in neighboring states where they could get it ups ground shipping for free anyway, because UPS got there in two days. It’s the second day, I guess. It was like two days shipping. So their ads all said, you know, free shipping. And it wasn’t they didn’t get an upgraded ship. They just got it two days, but they didn’t know that and it was insanely profitable. Like few hours of work insanely profitable. I was asked back like 60 days later, they had completely changed the entire thing back because they the different parts of the organization didn’t feel like they were getting their fair share. So they all got, you know, their fair share. And I just in the the owner sitting across the table from me goes, yeah, that ads don’t work for us. And I was so irritated. I said to him, I was like, No, you don’t know how to do you don’t know how to make ads work. I made ads work. You broke it. And he was like, he was doing sense. What are you talking about? I was like I said, because you’re optimizing for the wrong thing. And he looked at me puzzled, like you’re optimizing to make all the division heads in your business happy. So they got their fair share. And that doesn’t produce good financial results. I was optimizing for financial results. I win. Right? That’s the deal. You’re optimizing for the wrong thing. Now I’d love to tell the happy and the universe of the stories that he saw the light and realized I was so smart. I think he called me an asshole until I left shortly after we didn’t end well because people don’t like to hear that. So we’ll this weapon with caution. But I can tell you, there is no more powerful question as a client services professional, and no more powerful question to have in the back of your mind as an effective human being, than asking yourself at every opportunity, what am I optimizing for? Because I can tell you from experience, when I optimize for being right. Especially if it’s in a personal relationship, like with my spouse, that’s not don’t don’t optimize for being right. Cuz that’s not, that’s not the right thing to optimize for. Right? There are lots of like, figure out what you’re optimizing for, and, and make all of your decisions subordinate to that, and get your clients to do the same thing. Because what you’ll find with clients is what they tell you that they are optimizing for, and the the actual bullshit that happens, what they’re really optimizing for, generally have no relationship to one another. Like don’t say we’re optimizing for sales. And that’s it. It’s so we’re at a resort, we want to put heads in beds. And it’s like, right, except you’ve got all this politics that happens behind the scenes that undermines your results. So what are we really optimizing for? Okay, deep breath, things move on to the next one. Penny, I answered yours directly. I really appreciate that. So thank you. Shawn 1:19:07
I love this question. Love, love, love this question. Because it’s the There’s a wonderful book by George Leonard called Mastery. Every time someone. I’ve been a martial artist since I was 12. Every time somebody tests for black belt, at their testing, I give them a copy of mastery. And what George Leonard really did when he talked to that is we spend on the path to mastery, you spend most of your time on what he calls the plateau. You’re just at this, you don’t feel like you’re making any progress. It’s just you know, making the donuts one thing after the other day after day working on your craft, and then all of a sudden, boom, you’ve leveled up, and it seems like it happens. Like, obviously, all of a sudden, well what the hell just happened. But the reality is, it’s the it’s the day after day that’s getting you there and then you notice that big jump and now there’s a new plateau and like that, that’s a I have that in my mind, I think when I think about this, the central idea that if you’re, if you’re doing something that’s really important to you, when you’re on the path to mastery, you’re going to spend the overwhelming amount of time in that making the donuts zone, where you’re just doing the work. So if that’s true, then you want that experience to be positive. Like I don’t. I don’t want to think about things like, Alright, how can we make $100,000 next month? Like, that’s, to me? That’s not it. I mean, that could be part of it. If that for some reason that was meaningful, but it has to have the the qualifier of the experience, right? So how, how can we accomplish some outcome? How can we create this asset or do this thing, but not at the expense of something, that’s one way to think about it. But when I talk about experience, I think about it. So I’ll use the example Andre gave,

after we’re on the other side of lab, being real, and in the world, in elite being in the world, which will be you know, beginning of April, those things will be happening will be, then then we’ve we’ve checked all the boxes in our, what we intended to do in the first quarter, there will there is a there’s an important that we will take me to take some time to celebrate that, like not necessarily have to have a party, but April 7 is my birthday. I’m not like I need to get, you know, top three suggestions from Amanda and put her on a list. And I want I want to also in that process, I want to improve my writing, and and how I like there’s so much I want to experience in the creation of those assets that really for me, the asset, the outcome is just the vehicle for the experience. And that happens a lot for me, I really, I like to set up the tension of a point B, knowing that if I if I do that, well, the experience of getting there will light be up, it will be the things that I like to do. And for me, because I have this seemingly hardwired part of my being that I like to be up against it. Like I like to be up against my abilities, and feel like I’m stretching, not that I’m breaking, but that I’m stretching. And and that’s why I often will do things like if it takes six weeks, let’s shave a week off because that’ll that’ll drive the behavior if I’m trying to get a certain level and create a certain thing. I asked myself in advance like what? What could what would make that experience even better? What can I learn while I’m doing this? Like where do I think I could shore up some of my knowledge? Or where do I think I could like, come at this a different way. And it just it shows up like the the the manifesto for the art of email was a was a good example about that. Like I wrote the base for that. And it’s it was not my default writing style. Like where we introduced some characters. And then we we introduced some characters that were going to be the vehicle for an idea that wound its way throughout the entirety of it like I was right up against my abilities the entire time working on that I like that that’s experience like that, like, I want that in everything that I do. And that but that doesn’t mean that should be you, it just means that the things that light you up is for some people, what may light you up is, you know what if I, I want to do, I want to write 1000 words a day, every day for 30 days. So I’m going to do this thing that’s going to that will pull me toward that. And, and I’m not 1000 Word today person I write more sporadically, so that wouldn’t motivate. But some people may get motivated by that just step by step by step, each day doing a thing like checking the box, you did it, you feel good about it, it builds, like if that if that progression feels good for you make that your experience of the thing, I think there’s some intersection of outcome and experience where I think of the outcome being the vehicle, that allows me to have the experience that I’m going for, it’s not, the outcome matters far less to me, because the outcome in a weird way, is in service to the experience, that makes sense, I may not make any sense at all, happy birthday, by the way.

I love the citizen and interview that it’s this idea that we’re we’re constantly building up complexity, and then complexity hits a certain critical mass. And then from that complexity, you find a nuisance Felicity, and then you get that new simplicity. And then it builds up to even greater complexity. And then you bring it down to simplicity. Again, that’s what we’re doing the system right now, full disclosure is complex as hell. We’ve put four things together. That yes, that under underlying those four things are shared principles may play really well together. André 1:32:35
Yeah, well, we’ve, we’ve, we’ve spoken about the obvious ones, minimizing refunds, and, and, you know, stuff like that. But that doesn’t really get to the essence of what makes a or what represents one body’s a happy customer. And my thinking about this is constantly evolving. And more recently, it’s evolved again, so I won’t express it all here, because it’s still it’s, you know, it’s something that I’ve got to work with, in my, in my, in my monkey mind, and eventually, I’ll get some, some, some clarity. But you know, it’s happy, this thing called happy customer, you know, we have a whole bunch of customers on this call now and more customers inside of our business, and I think happy for all of them are, it’s going to be slightly different. But ultimately, there’s probably some, some things that are common. And I think one of them is, comes down to understanding. And again, these aren’t this, there’s not a spreadsheet for this, I don’t think I don’t think there is, or there can be a spreadsheet for this other than, than the obvious thing that that I’ve already mentioned. But, you know, understanding is something that’s, that’s nuanced. And again, understanding is going to be different for for everyone, everyone’s coming in with, with a different worldview and a different way of, of, of not just seeing the world through the lens, but seeing the business through that lens and seeing the work that we do through that lens. And then these, these, this, this attention, it’s, you know, any, every single one of our customers, their attention is pulled in a million different ways, you know, Andre and Shawn, we just we just to, to to nutjobs that that happened to be be somewhere in your sphere of influence, that you pay attention to some of the time. We would love to say you, you spend most of your attention on the work we do, but that’s not reality. So, I think you know, I think it comes down to really just understanding these these different dimensions that represent what they what they could be. You know, one example is, when I first myself, Mr. Mr. Original business partner with TLD one. So this is this is back in 2011, Steve gray and myself when we, we first created TLB. And we credit a course that that spanned over 11, seven months. So, so the less so somebody would make a purchase, and they would go through this training, and their curriculum took 11 months, because that’s how we felt, there was so much stuff, it was so dense that it would be, we felt it would be a disservice, to put it into a into this thing that will be accessible from day one. So that contributed to how we understood what a happy customer would be, it’s somebody that we could gradually take them, you know, just like somebody goes to university, there are given the entire curriculum dumped on her lap and say, there we go. Exams are in six months, you know, go do your best, you go through this thing, and it’s gradual. And the reason is because of, you know, understanding plays out in that way. So, that’s why we did that. And then it’s weird, we, we got to the end of of the training, and then we surveyed, one of our customers, and those that responded, the large majority of maybe a large majority is, is overstating it, but there was a, there was a significant portion of people that were still stuck on module one, which was how to find your, your niche, you know, that’s, that’s the wording that we that we use back then it’s your, your, this thing that you’re going to go and do and create in the world. And this is a segment of people that you’re going to go after, and these are your people. Well, people were stuck on that that part was so difficult for them, that the other six months of the training almost became irrelevant. And we only when you realize that after the fact

relative right at the end. So although we were with the best of intentions, we were trying to, we are trying to engineer a way to disseminate our training in a way that we were going to create a happy customer, we didn’t create a happy customer, for many of them is because they were still stuck right in the very beginning. And then nothing else mattered. So it’s just it’s things like that it’s understanding the nuances of a market. And that is not easy to really wrap your head around. Because there’s just so many dimensions to it. So I’m not being specific on purpose, because there’s, you can’t shine a light on this thing. It’s just a feeling. There needs to be a feedback loop where you’re interacting with your customers, you know, do does the attention you have that you command with those customers is that atrophying over time. And, you know, there’s obviously going to be some level of atrophy. But you know, what is it? I mean, I suppose you that’s can be measured and measured in some way. But yeah, it’s just under trying to define what those what those dimensions are and then really doing everything to try and and in trying to make those those those those elements were accessible and understanding is one I mean, we we’d be doing everybody a disservice if we just lump this on everyone. There’s a reason why we’re doing this q&a Call right is because we were trying to learn or understand What don’t you understand so then we can then go and put it inside there. And because without the level of understanding with any of our courses, let alone this thing called the system that we still busy trying to articulate. If somebody just is exposed to this and literally shut their pants, I’m sorry, but the the visual there but they just kind of bounce straight away. And although they may not refund because the level the perception of the stuff that they support that level of of awesomeness. It’s it’s clear it’s there, and that person may not refund so therefore you can’t even measured that element. But it just becomes we just become another statistic on the on the virtual bookshelf. And over time, maybe even a short period of time. We just We just are faulted out of that person’s attention to the point where even though they haven’t unsubscribed even though they haven’t refunded. We If we have lost the ability to communicate with them in a way that’s meaningful. And that’s, that’s a disservice. So that’s, again, that’s represents somebody that’s not a happy customer. So, I’m sorry, if I’m, if I’m skirting around the issue, but it is complicated. And I think it’s just, it’s just understanding that it is complicated, and that you do your absolutely best your absolute best to try and constantly increase the experience that somebody has with, with with this course that they’ve purchased, or this thing that they’ve the skill that they want to get better at. And on the other side of that, on the other side of the understanding is the doing component, and part of the doing component is getting a result. And these breakdowns can happen at any point, somebody can, conceptually I get this a love, you know, out of email, I understand this. And then they stare at their business. And there’s this fear that, oh, God, I’ve got to change everything, what I change, and that, that paralysis that comes from, from that fear. That’s also as doing a disservice. Because, again, we can’t be responsible for somebody else’s outcome, but we can be responsible, responsible to try and make it so accessible that it becomes a no brainer to, to take it and use it.

And ultimately try and get a result from it. And I think the ultimate, you know, checkbox for happy customer is somebody that’s understood enough of the, the training that matters to them. So obviously, the system is made up of a whole bunch of parts. And and this all comes together in this systematic thing, and people are going to be interacting with with different components, and they are going to choose certain things and put it in their business that makes sense and get a result. And if that’s a positive result, now we moving in the direction of happy customer. And I think out of all of our customers that have stayed with us the longest, and we still have the attention, are the ones that have understood enough, and then gone and put it into their business, even if it’s messy, and got a result. And then they just, that becomes a feedback loop. And they just run on that little on on their flywheel and we stay front and center, somebody worth paying attention to because they can see that the stuff works. So I think that’s my, that’s my longest answer.

Appreciate you making me feel better about saying fuck earlier. Thank you. It’s, I still love this question. For example, we initially intended to anyone who had three or more of our core courses, we initially intended to just upgrade them automatically to the system, right. And we we charged a nominal fee $95. Not because we wanted to make the $95. But because that actually filtered out about 70% of the people who didn’t even bother to raise their hand, it made some, it made a segment of our audience have to do something to take a step closer to us. Because we believe that’s likely to make a happier customer that at the end of the day, not getting it for free having to actually do just something made the difference. And we use this all the time in our thinking. Shawn 1:58:12

Alright, I think I’ll come at this a different way, which is, we’ve written about this a little bit, but it’s really foundational to a lot of successes I’ve had broadly defined, which is it’s an idea from Charlie Munger, about the importance of, of avoiding mistakes, versus trying to be innovative or smart. So I, I’ll be crystal clear. I was never innovative. When I ran an agency, especially on the traffic side of things. Nothing I did was like cutting edge at all. The thing that made me so successful, I didn’t know it at the time. It wasn’t until I think MFA live maybe I mentioned it on stage. I realize like I heard myself say it I was like Oh yeah, that was it. But the what made my me very successful on my client’s behalf was that there are 1015 or so at the time, like I had a list of 12 very specific mistakes that people made primarily. I was talking about this in the past, it was primarily around Google ads. There were 1212 mistakes that were very common and I wasn’t innovative, but my clients just didn’t make those 12 mistakes. And if you don’t make those mistakes and there’s a great metaphor from this for this, which is you can’t out exercise a box of doughnuts, meaning you can be the most jacked crossfitter in the world. You know, go to the we’ll call it a gyms and have your CrossFitters call up the box. Go to the box, like five days a week, smash it with your high intensity workouts. high five each other. Yeah, bro, smash it, smash it, smash it. And after every time you go to the CrossFit box, you eat a half dozen donuts, guess what? You’re not ever going to out exercise the mistake you’re making with the doughnuts. Right? So that’s, that’s mistake avoidance. Charlie Munger who’s Warren Buffett’s business partner describes the head there’s a book written in his honor about this one idea that’s that summarize it perfectly, which is all I ever want to know is where I’m going to die. So I never go there. Right? Like that’s, that’s the brilliance of this idea. So it’s, it’s partly comes from inversion, which is, uh, I think Jacoby is the person who’s the idea. It’s like, instead of trying to figure out how to do X, you try to figure out what would be involved in non x. And then you never do that, right? So most people will come and say, like, what, you know, what five steps should we take to have the most effective business in 2022? Right, and everyone comes up with ideas. Another way to do it is to say, hey, let’s figure out, let’s make a list of like, all the things we could do that would really undermine our success this year, and take it serious, like sit down, yeah, we can have really crappy customer support, we could win it like, like, make the list and then just commit to never doing any of that. Right? It sounds like it’s the same thing. But it’s not. And I don’t know why it’s not. There’s something very different about mistake avoidance, when you do it right about inversion, when you do it, right, that you get a result that you don’t get the other way. I don’t know if it’s how our minds are situated. I don’t know. I don’t understand it. But there’s something really weird when you sit down and ask yourself, it’s like it’s a it’s a weird permission. Like, I’m sure if I like I would love to do this in a conversation with Amanda. If I sat down as like, hey, Amanda, tell me tell me all like minimum 10 things that I could do that would really screw up my Facebook advertising results, like and like really think about it. And it asked me some questions and make my list and you really refine it, I could probably make a, let’s just assume that we come up with 10 that I can make a list of 10 things that if I just didn’t do those 10 things, I would probably be way ahead of my competitors who were likely doing some or all of those things, right. That’s how inversion works. So

I don’t I mean, you can go to Farnam Street, Shane Parrish, his blog, he has some articles on lots of stuff on Charlie Munger license. There’s a great interview with a guy who wrote one of my favorite books, which is, it’s his name’s Peter bevel. And there’s a great interview with Peter beveling, about this idea of mistake avoidance. Because when Andre and I talked about this, earlier this year, at the end of last year about it’s not about trying to be smart, it’s about trying to be less dumb. And it sounds like we’re being humble. But we don’t mean it. Like that’s not It’s not humility, it’s not false humility, it’s just recognizing that there’s so much we don’t know and it’s just it’s a it’s a each day as an opportunity to be just a little bit less dumb about different things. It feels different, there’s a different energy around that maybe that doesn’t motivate you and so people are far more motivated by the positive and they’re moving towards something and I’m, I tend to be more motivated away from like, not wanting for me, I’m motivated by not wanting to be done. And I’m done in so many ways and insects. So thoroughly dumb. If you want to feel dumb, like really in this I don’t want to be like why would I want to feel dumb? Because there’s no judgment? Right? There’s I don’t I say I feel dumb. I don’t feel bad about myself. I’m not like your jerks. I know I I just I know, their whole, their whole worlds I’m not first of all, not even aware of. So it’s not only do I not know what I don’t know, I don’t even know that. I don’t know it. If you want to if you want to feel as dumb as I do. Go listen to Mike to Sam Harris has listened to the audiobook, but it’s Sam Harris, his book Making sense. It’s 22 hours of his podcast with some of the biggest thinkers around some of the most complicated topics. And it’s just, it’s just moment after moment of I don’t Oh, my God, I don’t even know what they’re talking about. Like I don’t even know. It’s beautiful. It’s just a wonderful experience. So that’s a that’s tangential to the question that you asked. But it’s, it’s that idea has has really improved the quality of my life. Well beyond any measurement I could ever give it is just to ask myself in any situation. What are the How could I get this really wrong? And just I’m when I’m on my ID, this is personal. This is relationship stuff. This is professional. This is creative output. It’s everything but just to just identify early, how can I get this really wrong? And then just keep that in mind? Don’t do that. Is that if I just, if I don’t do that, at least my results aren’t going to be terrible, right? I don’t know. That’s that that would be my like contribution. Shawn 2:09:13
it’s funny too, because I, I I started as you were talking, I started making my answer far more nuanced than it actually is. Like I wanted people to feel comfortable with that. Any of whether or not it’s you know, it’s the the question is framed, you know, happy customers, no hard persuasion, implicit in that is that there’s a possibility that the money back guarantee is falls under the category of hard persuasion tactics. But that’s implicit in this question. And I think, the way I would answer that question, it’s a quote I love from Zig Ziglar. I’m a big student of Zig Ziglar. And I don’t, I don’t know enough about it. I sort of know who he was and other things, but there’s something Zig Ziglar said that many others Jay Abraham and others have really expanded on. But the original quote from Zig Ziglar is that if you believe your product or service can fulfill a true need, it’s your moral obligation to sell it. Now that’s like, I think about that a lot as we both embrace strategy of preeminence Jai RAM strategy of preeminence. And when you when you take it to its logical conclusion, its explicit logical conclusion, as described by Jay Abraham, you, you have to there’s a part missing in the description that I think we’ve we’ve filled in, which is important that all fill it, which is you, when you if you truly believe that you’re in, you’re not fooling yourself. But if you’re, if you’re putting your heart and soul into something, and you believe and you have evidence to support, that this truly works, it truly fulfills a need, it truly can change people’s lives. If they do their part. I do believe you have some responsibility to for the people who should have it, to have it. Now, where do we draw the line? And you know, we don’t love the hard persuasion stuff. But make no mistake, we still persuade, right? We’re not we’re not reading the books on persuasion, we’re not using those tactics, but we write in ways that are pulling the right people toward that outcome, that’s we’re deliberately because we believe what we’ve created changes lives. If we didn’t, then all bets are off, right? Like, then you cross into the unethical side of things, but, but then the ethics around the crappy product, not how you sell it. Like, it’s, it’s not a sell, it’s that everything’s a system, right? It’s just it’s the relationship. So if you if you have a really good product, and you’ve got the right audience in front of that product, and and you pull out all the stops to sell it. Is that okay? I feel pretty good about that. But if the product is crappy, and the the audience is wrong, and those parts aren’t synergistically lining up, then then you can’t apply the same logic in the same statement. So I, I think I land on this, of being

it’s not persuasion in the in the service of the buyers best interest is okay with me. But never but we we feel, we feel it when it’s off. And we don’t go there with it feels like too much. And the abundant Money Back Guarantee didn’t feel like too much. Because we felt like more than anything. It was something we had to do, that we were making people aware of that was available to them. And I wanted them to feel like if they did take the chance and go you know what, this was not for me that everything was okay. They could just let us know. And no problem. Like it’s not a big deal. And to start up do you leverage any affiliate networks to drive traffic to your own systems? I’m going to answer that and I will you answer that and I’m just going to go on to the next one after you’re done answering

André 2:14:42
Yeah, we we don’t leverage affiliate networks. In fact, we’re we’re we’ve manually turned ourselves so because we’re on the ClickBank marketplace while we we sell through Clickbank there are there are retailer so they’ve got a huge marketplace where you can go and search for For four products that if you’re an affiliate looking looking for offers to promote, you can go to the ClickBank marketplace, do a search under a certain category and all the things come up, it’s got the conversion rates, and all that stuff. And then most most, most affiliates will then just sort by the top three products that make the most money and off they go, and they just throw traffic at it. So for that reason, we’re we’re have unavailable on that network, because we don’t want junk traffic. And I think you can build a business that can make a lot of money from shitty traffic from affiliate networks, and many people have got rich from that. It’s, you know, make no mistake, it’s a full time job. And there’s elements have been, you know, gray, unethical, because it’s just, it’s just, it’s just pumping bits. You know, it’s it’s not about relationships. So I think that’s thinking about that we would rather handpick audiences. So we’ll reach out to certain people like, like Amanda bond, for example, you know, if her audience is a really good fit for our stuff, or then we will, we’ll, you know, do a deal with with Amanda. And that’s, that’s a win win for both of us, and she endorses our stuff, and we all make money, and we get really good prospects coming in, and really good customers coming in. And that’s more meaningful for us. But I don’t think you find that on. I’m generalizing here, but I don’t think you can find that on affiliate networks. Unless your offer is very broad luck. Make Money Online broad, like, click buttons and you know, get rich, tomorrow broad, then you can go to the affiliate networks. And again, that’s just a different game. And that’s, that’s not the game we’re in. So we don’t.

Shawn 2:28:39
Yeah, I can’t remember where we talked about this. But we have, which is the the short answer is yes. Because the economics of traffic change. So fundamentally, that the thing that conversion rates that used to work no longer work. So you know, back in the day, when you could, you could get one cent clicks on Google all day long, then you could have your business model could work when you have a lot of cheap traffic, but there really isn’t any cheap traffic anymore. And that those days have kind of gone I mean, you can get less expensive traffic, but the one cent click days are over that are not coming back. So and traffic costs of red have risen steadily over time. And in to be clear, when I say traffic costs these most people are paying most people pay for traffic at some stage. And most large businesses pay for essentially all of their traffic, because it’s predictable, and it’s dependable. So if you’re getting a ton of organic traffic, you’re like, What is he talking about your traffic cost? Well, the game being played by most of the big players, you have to pay for traffic because you just need a lot of it. And when it’s not cheap anymore. 1% half percent quarter percent conversion rates don’t work. And that has had seismic repercussions throughout the internet marketing world. that as traffic becomes more expensive, you need much higher conversion rates just to break even in much more sophisticated business models, just to break even. And you have to change the economic window of your business over longer periods of time. To compensate for the fact that most people don’t buy initially, they buy later they buy, you know, beyond 90 days to two years, like you have to, you have to take all of that, and really what, what our what our, our model of shame, I think about a man or framework, how we think about digital marketing really, is it the thing that we do that I think we do better than most other people do. And I don’t, it’s not a humble brag, or that’s just, I think it’s a intellectually defensible statement. But that we understand we’re willing to talk about the unspoken realities that many others are either unaware of, like the implications of them, or they’re aware of, but they don’t want to explicitly talk about it. So things like traffic costs are going up. Conversion rates don’t generally happen in the first 30 days that most of them like we these are all parts of reality. So when we look at all of reality, and build methods that that reflect that reality like that, does that make sense? Right? It’s not. So a lot of it doesn’t mean that the the other ways of doing direct response don’t work or didn’t work, it just means they’re becoming harder and harder. And when you spend any time with the big direct response marketers, it’s all the same story, which is, it’s getting harder and harder to get high quality traffic, there’s more competition, people are yelling louder. The signal to noise ratio is dramatically in favor of noise. All of those things are true. And those truths are, what the implications of those truths are what inform what we teach in the system. And how we approach the market means that we, we recognize the implications of what’s true. And we create strategies that work with those realities, not fighting against them. Like that answers. I hope that answers the question. And yes, people are seeing this. Absolutely. Seeing this. If you think it’s like a two part Venn diagram, there are an enormous number of people that need branding and marketing website. And then there’s an enormous number of people who want to learn how to update websites. But the overlap is a much smaller sliver of people who need both. And that’s the part that you have to validate like, how do you find because it’s really in be cautious with it, because you’re moving down? This is not this is the antithesis of the Reese’s Peanut Butter Cup or chocolate peanut butter great together, I think you the I think you want to sell the companion online course of my memories correct from previous conversations, your WordPress developer. So I think you want to actually validate the getting the most from your WordPress site online course as in in market that too, because then you have a much larger audience, sell that to people who already have a website, and they want to get more out of it, they want to do that work themselves. Because if you don’t do that, it’s really hard to understand which part of your business is that that needs your attention. Because you can have people who are, they don’t want to build a website, right now they want to work on their own website. So you’re not attractive to them. Because your course is about both. So when you separate them, you get a much when you pull, especially the second part, and you pull the course out of the mix. And people can do the website and the companion course. Or if they already have a website, they can they can learn how to get the most from it, you get a much larger audience and a much truer signal to validate the offer. So that’s that’s how I would think about it. You’ve added some notes here, Andre will jump into this while I read some of the notes that you’ve added over here.

André 2:46:27
Yeah, so limit come at this at a few ways. I mean, it is it is competitive, right? As soon as you talk about websites, you certainly can’t. I think it’s difficulty advertise because all the big brands or the big guys are going to be ranking for anything to do with websites or website themes. It’s just a never ending. Pit of deep competitive. But so I think that’s the bad news. But there’s always good news, there’s there’s always a way to to to find opportunities in competitive spaces. And again, as I said earlier on. Competition validates the market. It does make it difficult sometimes and challenging to find those opportunities. So I’ll express one way to do this. From our buddy, Dean Jackson, I think he I haven’t met haven’t come across anybody who has solved this particular issue. Well, the solution he’s, he’s, he’s, he’s made this work in a way that is so unique. And, and it just, it just worked so well. And so I’ll give you the 10,000 foot view. As he’s explained in some of his podcasts, I mean, just go to Isla marketing.com. He’s, he’s got a podcast today with with Joe Polish. And it’s not one of those, you know, just it’s one of those. But anyway, he he unpacks how he does so Dean Jackson really broadly, he’s he started off in the real estate, I think, I think that’s a real estate space. So he has this, this pool of, of appliance, customers. So he has an audience already. And one of the things one of the things that he solved is this website thing much, much like you’ve you’ve you’ve, you’ve explained. So what Dean did, does is he is he created an entire little turnkey websites for his clients. So they get everything, they get the entire all the assets, they they get the ability to plug this thing in, and it’ll generate leads, they just need to fill in their details. It’s not necessarily a template thing, but I’m sure I’ve never seen it myself. But as he’s explains it, it sounds like there’s a template component to it, you just, you just create it, you just edit it. So it fits your business. But it’s essentially you’re in the retail leads business. He gives you this thing for free. I think it’s for free. And it’s yours, you get to you plug it in, and you drive traffic to it and generate leads, those are your leads. And I think it’s like maybe after 60 days, after 90 days, whatever it is, or when when the first lead comes in and starts it’s it’s one of the two, I forget the nuances. But then he then he charges them. The only cost to them is the $99 a month. And again, I might have the number wrong but seem to remember $99 a month. So he’s got a continuity product that he’s created out of thin air from giving people so he’s done all the work to build this asset to test it. He knows it works. And then he’s then used to selling this thing over Whenever again to all his real estate clients, and he’s got a, and this is just one component of his business, which is I think it’s substantial. I don’t know if it’s if it’s 1000s of customers or paying a nine bucks a month. But that’s, that’s amazing. When I first heard that a few years ago, I thought, god damn, that’s, that’s good. So anyway, that’s how he solved the problem in a really, really competitive space.

Like whatever anonymous earlier asked like, the real, like, the real way to do things, the real way to do things is to figure out what you’re trying to accomplish with accomplish for you, and your customers and subordinate all decision making. to that.
